quick_facts.label: What it is
quick_facts.value: “State TET” is a family of state or Union Territory teacher-eligibility tests conducted by the appropriate state authority under the national TET framework; it is not one single all-India examination.

quick_facts.label: Teaching levels
quick_facts.value: The national framework distinguishes Paper I for prospective teachers of Classes I–V and Paper II for Classes VI–VIII. A state’s notification may use its own category names and add state-specific school-level categories.

quick_facts.label: Eligibility baseline
quick_facts.value: For elementary teaching, NCTE’s minimum-qualification notification combines an approved academic/teacher-education route with passing TET; the exact acceptable route and conditions must be checked in the relevant state notification.

quick_facts.label: Passing is not appointment
quick_facts.value: A TET pass is an eligibility criterion, not an automatic right to recruitment or employment. Candidates must separately satisfy the applicable recruitment advertisement, document verification, reservation, subject, language, and other service-rule requirements.

quick_facts.label: State-specific logistics
quick_facts.value: Application dates, fee, media/languages, exam date, paper pattern, qualifying concessions, certificate handling, and recognition rules vary by state and cycle. For example, Maharashtra’s official June 2026 portal lists separate Paper I/Paper II fees and a postponement notice, illustrating why the current notification must be checked rather than relying on a generic TET timetable.

sections.heading: Purpose and scope
paragraphs: State TETs are eligibility examinations for elementary-school teaching. Under the NCTE framework, the appropriate government conducts TET for teacher appointment eligibility in Classes I–VIII; the state test normally serves that state’s government, local-authority, and relevant school recruitment ecosystem. The precise applicability of a certificate across employers or states is governed by the applicable state rules and recruitment notice.

Passing State TET should be treated as one step in a teacher-employment pathway rather than as a job offer. The national guidance says that school managements may give weight to TET scores in recruitment, but qualifying TET alone does not create a right to recruitment or employment.
bullets: Use State TET when targeting elementary-teacher roles covered by the particular state’s recruitment rules.
Use the current state notification—not coaching-site summaries—to confirm whether a state TET, CTET, or another accepted certificate is valid for the vacancy.
For private-school roles, employer requirements can differ, although TET may be required or preferred where the applicable rules make it relevant.

sections.heading: Eligibility
paragraphs: Eligibility is state- and paper-specific. NCTE’s published minimum-qualification framework for Classes I–V includes routes such as Senior Secondary plus a recognised elementary-teacher-education qualification, B.El.Ed., special-education routes, or graduation plus D.El.Ed.; for Classes VI–VIII it includes routes such as graduation plus D.El.Ed. or B.Ed., alongside other specified teacher-education routes. These national provisions have amendments and state-level implementation details, so candidates should match their qualification, marks, category, language and training status to the live state notice.

A current state implementation can be more operationally specific. For example, Maharashtra’s official MAHATET FAQ distinguishes Paper I (Standards 1–5) from Paper II (Standards 6–8) and lists different educational/professional qualification combinations for each. This is an example, not a substitute for another state’s notification.
bullets: Choose Paper I for the primary level, Paper II for the upper-primary level, or both only where your qualifications and target roles support both.
Confirm recognition/status of D.El.Ed., B.Ed., B.El.Ed., special-education, or equivalent credentials before applying.
Check category-related concessions, age conditions if any, domicile/language requirements, and rules for candidates whose teacher-education course is ongoing in the specific notification.

sections.heading: Exam format
paragraphs: The NCTE TET guideline sets a two-paper elementary structure: Paper I for Classes I–V and Paper II for Classes VI–VIII. Its baseline structure uses objective multiple-choice questions, with child development and pedagogy, language, and mathematics/environmental studies in Paper I; Paper II includes child development and pedagogy, languages, and either mathematics/science or social studies. States may operationalise this framework through their own syllabus, language media, categories, timings and instructions.

The guideline describes 150 multiple-choice questions and a 90-minute duration for each paper, but candidates should treat those as framework-level reference points. The current state prospectus/admit-card instructions control the actual pattern, duration, accessibility accommodations, language options and permitted materials for that cycle.
bullets: Download the state’s latest syllabus and paper-wise scheme before beginning preparation.
Prepare for the pedagogy component as well as content knowledge; it is part of both elementary-level papers in the national framework.
Use only the official current-year paper pattern when planning time allocation.

sections.heading: Application process
paragraphs: The typical process is online: read the notification and eligibility rules, create a registration/login, complete the application, upload or enter the required information, pay the applicable fee, submit, and preserve the confirmation/printout. Maharashtra’s official FAQ documents this sequence for MAHATET, while Kerala’s K-TET portal directs applicants to read the notification, prospectus, general information and application guidelines before submission.

Dates and fees are cycle-specific. As a dated illustration only, Maharashtra’s June 2026 portal listed an online application window ending on April 24, 2026 and displayed separate fees for one paper versus both papers; its homepage also displayed a notice that the June 2026 examination had been postponed. Candidates must rely on the live portal for revised dates and should not infer a nationwide schedule from this example.
bullets: Apply only through the designated official state examination portal.
Save the submitted form, payment receipt, application number and login credentials.
Check the portal again for correction windows, admit-card availability, centre details, answer-key objections, result and certificate/document-verification instructions.

sections.heading: Preparation
paragraphs: Begin with the official paper-wise syllabus and model questions for the state and category selected. Kerala’s K-TET portal publishes category-specific syllabi and model-question links, and the MAHATET portal provides an official syllabus, question sets and answer keys. These materials are more reliable than assuming that another state’s topic distribution, terminology or language medium will apply.

Build preparation around the prescribed school-level content plus pedagogy: child development, inclusive learning, assessment, language pedagogy, and subject pedagogy. Then practise timed multiple-choice papers and use official answer keys or model questions to identify recurring weak areas. Recheck the notification shortly before the examination for changes to instructions or schedule.
bullets: Map every official syllabus unit to a study plan before buying materials or joining coaching.
Practise in the language medium selected for the examination whenever official materials are available.
Review official previous question sets, model questions and answer keys; do not treat unofficial answer keys as final.

sections.heading: Outcomes after qualifying
paragraphs: A qualifying State TET result can establish the TET component of eligibility for relevant elementary-teaching recruitment in that state, subject to the certificate’s applicable recognition and the vacancy rules. It can support progression toward primary or upper-primary teaching roles in state-government, local-authority, aided, or private-school settings where the relevant employer and rules accept it.

It does not replace the rest of recruitment. A candidate may still need to meet the post’s academic and professional qualifications, category and document requirements, language/subject conditions, selection-test or merit rules, and employer-specific conditions. The NCTE guidance expressly characterises TET as only one eligibility criterion for appointment.
bullets: Track teacher-recruitment notices separately after qualifying; the TET result itself is not a recruitment application.
Keep the certificate/result, qualification records, identity documents and category certificates ready for verification.
Before applying outside the issuing state, confirm acceptance of that State TET in the specific employer’s notification.
